county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1787 from Bedford County. Named for the Countess of Huntingdon. Located in the Juniata Valley. Iron furnace industry significant. Pennsylvania Canal passed through county. Juniata College (1876) in Huntingdon. Agricultural and industrial mix.
county_metadata.research_tips: Huntingdon County Historical Society maintains genealogical files and iron industry records. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1787. For pre-1787 check Bedford County. Iron furnace records are important for worker documentation. Pennsylvania Canal records document transportation workers and merchants. Juniata College archives have local history. Scotch-Irish and German settlers are documented in Presbyterian and Lutheran church records. The Standing Stone area has pre-colonial Native American heritage. Broad Top coal mining area has separate industrial heritage. Raystown Lake area has submerged community records preserved at historical society.
county_metadata.heritage_book: History of Huntingdon and Blair Counties (1883)

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1803 from Westmoreland and Lycoming counties. Named for the Indiana Territory. 'Christmas Tree Capital of the World.' Bituminous coal mining significant. Indiana University of Pennsylvania (1875). Birthplace of Jimmy Stewart. Agricultural with coal mining heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Indiana County Historical and Genealogical Society in Indiana maintains extensive collections including family files and cemetery transcriptions. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1803. For pre-1803 check Westmoreland and Lycoming counties. Coal mining records document immigrant miners from Eastern Europe and Italy. The university (IUP) archives have local history. Scotch-Irish and German settlers documented in Presbyterian and Lutheran records. The Saltsburg area connects to Conemaugh River trade. Jimmy Stewart Museum has Stewart family history. Christmas tree farming heritage is unique. Check Armstrong and Cambria county records for cross-border families.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation.
Wills, administrations, estate inventories from 1803.

source_profiles.source_name: PA State Archives — Indiana County Marriages

Federal census from 1810. Coal miners documented from mid-1800s.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Scotch-Irish Presbyterian and German Lutheran records. Later Catholic parishes for immigrant miners.
Indiana Gazette and local papers with coal industry and community coverage.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1804 from Lycoming County. Named for Thomas Jefferson. Forested mountain county. Timber and glass industries. Bituminous coal mining. Punxsutawney is famous for Groundhog Day. DuBois area borders Clearfield County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County History Center in Brookville maintains genealogical files. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1830 (county organized 1830). For pre-1804 check Lycoming County. Timber industry records document early settlers. Glass factory records in Brookville area. The Punxsutawney area has distinct settlement patterns tied to mining. Lutheran, Presbyterian, and Methodist records are primary. Cross-reference with Clearfield and Elk counties. The Mahoning Creek valley connects to Armstrong County families. DuBois area industrial records overlap with Clearfield County.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county organization.
Wills, administrations from 1830.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage license applications from 1885.
Federal census from 1810.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Methodist, Presbyterian, and Lutheran church records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1831 from Mifflin County. Named for the Juniata River. Agricultural county in the Juniata Valley. Pennsylvania Canal passed through. Small, rural county with strong German and Scotch-Irish heritage. Tuscarora State Forest borders western edge.
county_metadata.research_tips: Juniata County Historical Society in Mifflintown maintains genealogical files and local records. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1831. For pre-1831 check Mifflin County. The Juniata River valley settlement patterns are key. German Reformed, Lutheran, and Presbyterian church records are primary sources. The Pennsylvania Canal era brought workers and merchants. Agricultural records document farming families. Cross-reference with Mifflin and Perry counties for family connections. Tuscarora Academy records in Academia document early education. The Amish and Mennonite communities in the county have separate record-keeping traditions.
county_metadata.heritage_book: History of That Part of the Susquehanna and Juniata Valleys (1886)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation.
Wills, administrations from 1831.

Federal census from 1840 (1830 under Mifflin).

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1878 from Luzerne County, the last county created in PA. Named for the Lackawanna River. Scranton is the major city. Anthracite coal mining and iron/steel industry dominated. Massive immigration: Welsh, Irish, Italian, Polish, Lithuanian, Ukrainian. One of PA's most ethnically diverse counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lackawanna Historical Society in Scranton has extensive collections documenting the anthracite region's ethnic diversity. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1878. For pre-1878 families check Luzerne County. The ethnic diversity requires checking multiple parish records: Welsh Congregational and Methodist, Irish Catholic (St. Patrick's), Italian Catholic (multiple parishes), Polish Catholic (multiple parishes), Lithuanian, Ukrainian, and Jewish synagogue records. The University of Scranton Weinberg Memorial Library has local history. Anthracite Heritage Museum documents mining culture. Scranton Iron Furnaces NHS records. Steamtown NHS has railroad worker records. The Scranton Public Library has genealogical materials and city directories from 1880.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Commemorative Biographical Record of Northeastern Pennsylvania (1900)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1878; online index. Pre-1878 at Luzerne County

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1729 from Chester County, the first county formed after the original three. Lancaster was the state capital 1799-1812. Heart of Pennsylvania Dutch (Deutsch) country. Amish, Mennonite, and other Plain communities. German immigration massive from 1700s. Agricultural powerhouse. Rich in church records from multiple German denominations.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lancaster County is one of the most genealogically significant counties in America due to its Pennsylvania Dutch heritage. The Lancaster County Historical Society (LancasterHistory) has extraordinary collections including family files, church records, and the Lancaster County Archives which houses deeds, wills, and court records. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1729. For pre-1729 check Chester County. The Amish and Mennonite communities maintain their own genealogical records; the Lancaster Mennonite Historical Society holds extensive collections. German Reformed and Lutheran church records are available on FamilySearch and in published form. The Moravian Archives in Bethlehem and Lititz Moravian Congregation records are important. James Buchanan's Wheatland estate papers document the 15th president's family. The Heritage Center of Lancaster County has material culture documentation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1729; LANDEX online system. Among oldest county deed records in PA

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1729. LANDEX online system. Among oldest county records in PA.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Extensive German Reformed, Lutheran, Moravian, Mennonite, and Amish baptism, marriage, burial records. The richest German church record collection in PA.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Exceptional preservation. Published in multiple volumes.

Original warrants, surveys, patents. Lancaster County had massive early land grants.
Lancaster Intelligencer, Lancaster New Era. German-language papers critical for PA Dutch research.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1849 from Beaver and Mercer counties. Named for the USS Lawrence (War of 1812). New Castle is major city. Steel and tin plate manufacturing. Significant Italian and Eastern European immigration. Fireworks capital of America (pyrotechnics industry).
county_metadata.research_tips: Lawrence County Historical Society in New Castle maintains genealogical files and industrial heritage records. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1849. For pre-1849 check Beaver and Mercer counties. Steel and tin plate industry records document immigrant workers. The Italian community in New Castle is one of the largest in western PA; check Catholic parish records (especially St. Vitus). The pyrotechnics industry generated unique business records. Presbyterian and Methodist records serve earlier families. Cross-reference with Mercer and Beaver counties. The Shenango Valley Industrial Museum has manufacturing records.

source_profiles.repository: Lawrence County Government Center, New Castle

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1813 from Dauphin and Lancaster counties. Pennsylvania Dutch country. Iron industry from colonial era (Cornwall Iron Furnace, 1742). Agricultural county. Strong German heritage. Fort Indiantown Gap military reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lebanon County Historical Society maintains genealogical files and iron industry records including Cornwall Iron Furnace documentation. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1813. For pre-1813 check Dauphin and Lancaster counties. German Reformed and Lutheran church records are critical; the county is solidly PA Dutch. Cornwall Iron Furnace (now museum) records document iron workers from 1742. Fort Indiantown Gap military records available through National Guard Bureau. The Annville/Palmyra area has distinct settlement patterns. Lebanon Valley College archives have local history. Hershey family connections to Lebanon County. Check Mennonite and Brethren community records for Plain families.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1813. Online index.
Wills, administrations from 1813.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Published church record collections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1812 from Northampton County. Named for the Lehigh River. Allentown is third-largest city in PA. German heritage dominant. Lehigh Valley industrial region. Cement, iron, and steel industries. Bethlehem Steel Company operations extended into Lehigh. Muhlenberg College and Cedar Crest College.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lehigh County Historical Society in Allentown has extensive genealogical collections. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1812. For pre-1812 check Northampton County. The German heritage dominates; check German Reformed, Lutheran, and Moravian church records. The Lehigh Valley was heavily industrialized; cement and steel company records document immigrant workers. Muhlenberg College (named for the Lutheran patriarch) archives have local manuscripts. The Allentown area attracted Irish, Italian, and Eastern European immigrants for industry. Liberty Bell was hidden in Allentown during the Revolution. Lehigh County Authority records document infrastructure development.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1812. Online index.
Wills, administrations from 1812.

Morning Call and predecessors with industrial and community coverage.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1786 from Northumberland County. Major anthracite coal region. Wilkes-Barre is the county seat. Site of the Wyoming Massacre (1778). Connecticut-Pennsylvania border dispute (Yankee-Pennamite Wars). Massive immigration for coal mining: Welsh, Irish, Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Italian, Ukrainian. Third-most populous PA county historically.
county_metadata.research_tips: Luzerne County Historical Society in Wilkes-Barre has extensive genealogical collections documenting the anthracite region. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1786. For pre-1786 check Northumberland County. The Wyoming Valley had Connecticut settlers under Susquehanna Company claims; check CT records for pre-1786 families. Coal mining generated massive immigration; check ethnic parish records in multiple languages. Welsh mining communities documented in Congregational and Baptist records. Polish Catholic parishes (multiple in Wilkes-Barre, Nanticoke, Pittston) have extensive records. King's College and Wilkes University archives have local history. The Knox Mine Disaster (1959) records document mining families. Osterhout Free Library has genealogical materials. The ethnic diversity makes this one of PA's most complex genealogical counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1786; online index. Includes Wyoming Valley colonial claims

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1786. Online index. Wyoming Valley land dispute records.
